config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically take priority over individual rights, because that can easily become an excuse for abusing people in the name of the “greater good.” In my view, a just society protects individual rights first, while still allowing limits when there is a clear and serious public need, like safety or preventing harm. From my perspective, collective interests matter, but they should not erase the dignity and freedom of the person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From a United States perspective, that kind of statement makes me wary because history shows how easily “the public good” can be used to justify censorship, discrimination, or government overreach. Society does have legitimate needs, like public safety and preventing harm, but those limits should be narrow and carefully justified rather than treated as a blank check. In my view, individual rights are the default, and society has to earn any exception.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over individual rights as a general rule, because in Indonesia I have seen how easily “for the common good” can be used to silence weaker people or excuse unfair treatment. At the same time, I do not believe individual rights should be absolute in every situation, since public order, disaster response, and protecting others from harm can justify real limits. The key point is that those limits must be narrow, transparent, and accountable, not just assumed because a majority wants something. A healthy society protects people first and asks the individual to compromise only when there is a clear, necessary, and proportionate reason.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, that idea sets off alarms because it can too easily become a justification for government overreach, censorship, or treating unpopular people as expendable in the name of the majority. That said, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every case; public safety, preventing harm, and emergency response can justify narrow limits when they are clearly necessary and proportionate. The burden should be on society to prove why a right must be limited, not on the individual to constantly defend why they deserve basic freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over individual rights as a general principle. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how “for the common good” can be used to pressure ordinary people, especially those with less power, so I am very cautious about giving society the upper hand by default. At the same time, I do not support an absolute view of individual rights either, because public safety, disaster response, and preventing real harm sometimes require limits. The right balance is to protect individual dignity first and only restrict it when the need is clear, necessary, and accountable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From my United States perspective, once you say society comes first by default, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to excuse censorship, discrimination, or other abuses in the name of the public good. I do accept that rights can be limited in serious cases like public safety or preventing harm, but those limits should be narrow, necessary, and strongly justified. The burden should stay on society to prove why a right must be restricted, because individual rights are what keep the majority from trampling minorities.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
